coredata.dc:title: Unlocking the potential of chlorophyll-based carbon dots towards water-splitting, white-light LED and encryption applications
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Pricilla, R. Blessy
Guler, Ali Can
Urbanek, Pavel
Urbanek, Michal
Jelinek, Raz
Hanulikova, Barbora
Kuritka, Ivo
coredata.dc:description: The versatile traits of carbon dots (CD) have positioned them as a compelling subject of study across various burgeoning fields. This work has successfully sourced CD from a porphyrin-containing compound, chlorophyll, which boasts outstanding light-harvesting and energy transfer capabilities. CD with excellent physicochemical properties were synthesized using a low-cost solvothermal method. The optical properties of CD have been harnessed for water-splitting applications with ZnO, resulting in significantly improved current efficiency of the photoanodes. When irradiated with AM1.5 G illumination, ZnO/CD heterojunction provided the highest photocurrent efficiency (0.225 mA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs RHE) as a result of enhanced light absorption across the UV to the visible region and improved interfacial charge transfer kinetics in comparison to the pristine ZnO. Additionally, the nanocomposite prepared of CD and boric acid (BACD) evinced promising naked eye phosphorescence for 10 s with an average phosphorescence lifetime of 1.5 s. The BACD nanocomposite was used as a free-standing thin film phosphor material for white light LED preparation and as an ink for data encryption. The results demonstrate that the acquired CD hold substantial promise for a variety of applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Photoanode
Nanocomposite
Phosphorescence
LED

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are happy to acknowledge the support given by the Ministry of Education, Youth and Sports of the Czech Republic DKRVO (RP/CPS/2024-28/007). Ali Can G\u00FCler also expresses his gratitude for support within the \u201CCreativity, Intelligence & Talent for the Zl\u00EDn Region\u201D (CIT - ZK) program.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) Extraction of chlorophyll and b) synthesis of CD from chlorophyll.
(a) TEM image of the CD and (b) particle size distribution of CD.
a) FTIR spectrum of CD and b) absorption spectrum and emission spectra of CD and chlorophyll at 380 nm.

Analysis of the PL emission intensity maximum wavelength dependency on the excitation light wavelength (as obtained from the data in Fig. S2). The data is plotted in photon energy scales, enabling linear extrapolation to the zero energy difference between the excitation and emission maximum intensity light photons.
a) Fluorescence and b) phosphorescence decay analysis for CD dispersed in water.

a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of BACD and heated boric acid, b) phosphorescence emission spectra of BACD and heated boric acid at excitation 302 nm, and c) naked eye phosphorescence of BACD at 302 nm d) Phosphorescence decay analysis for BACD – excited at 300 nm, phosphorescence emission recorded at 550 nm.
TEM image of a) ZnO nanorods and b) CD on ZnO nanorods.
a) UV–Vis absorption spectra and b) PL emission spectra (excitation wavelength 350 nm) of ZnO/CD and ZnO thin films.
a) The light-copped LSV curves, b) LSV curves of ZnO/CD with different impregnation times in CD solution, and c) photostability of pristine ZnO and ZnO/CD thin films.

a) EIS data in the Nyquist plots in the presence of irradiation (the inset is the equivalent circuit model used for fitting), b) IPCE at an applied bias of 1.23 V vs. RHE, and c) ABPE efficiencies of ZnO, and ZnO/CD.
Schematic illustration of proposed PEC water splitting mechanism via ZnO/CD photoanode.

a) BACD as a phosphor layer on 310 nm LED with different thicknesses, and b) chromaticity diagram indicating the color coordinates of the CD of different thicknesses (0.454 mm (A), 0.636 mm (B) and 0.819 mm (C)) on LED diode 310 nm.
Information encryption using of BACD ink through a microarray data storage pattern according to the binary codes.

head.ce:title: Unlocking the potential of chlorophyll-based carbon dots towards water-splitting, white-light LED and encryption applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre of Polymer Systems, Tomas Bata University in Zlín, Trida Tomase Bati 5678, Zlin, 76001, Czech Republic
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centre of Polymer Systems, Tomas Bata University in Zlin, trida Tomase Bati 5678, Zlin 76001, Czech Republic,

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Ben Gurion University of the Negev, Beer Sheva, 8410501, Israel

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ben Gurion University of the Negev

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Ben Gurion University of the Negev, Beer Sheva 8410501, Israel

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The versatile traits of carbon dots (CD) have positioned them as a compelling subject of study across various burgeoning fields. This work has successfully sourced CD from a porphyrin-containing compound, chlorophyll, which boasts outstanding light-harvesting and energy transfer capabilities. CD with excellent physicochemical properties were synthesized using a low-cost solvothermal method. The optical properties of CD have been harnessed for water-splitting applications with ZnO, resulting in significantly improved current efficiency of the photoanodes. When irradiated with AM1.5 G illumination, ZnO/CD heterojunction provided the highest photocurrent efficiency (0.225 mA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs RHE) as a result of enhanced light absorption across the UV to the visible region and improved interfacial charge transfer kinetics in comparison to the pristine ZnO. Additionally, the nanocomposite prepared of CD and boric acid (BACD) evinced promising naked eye phosphorescence for 10 s with an average phosphorescence lifetime of 1.5 s. The BACD nanocomposite was used as a free-standing thin film phosphor material for white light LED preparation and as an ink for data encryption. The results demonstrate that the acquired CD hold substantial promise for a variety of applications.

The chlorophyll derived carbon dots was successfully synthesized using the cost effective hydrothermal method. The carbon dots were sensitized on ZnO and used as an effective photoanode providing a photocurrent of 0.225 mA/cm2. The carbon dots in boric acid matrix were also used for LED and information encryption applications. Unique phosphorescence characteristics of carbon dots in boric acid matrix provided naked eye phosphorescence of 10s with an average phosphorescence lifetime of 1.5 s.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CD), discovered in 2004, are one of the most promising carbon-based nanomaterials due to their dimensions of less than 10 nm [1]. Diverse approaches to their synthesis enable them to achieve a multitude of compelling functionalities [2]. Moreover, they can be prepared from renewable natural sources – plants, such as leaves, fruit peels or flowers, structural characteristics of which can be given to CD as an imprint of their original molecules predetermining them for specific applications [3]. The fascinating assets of the green and sustainable CD [4] make them well-suited for a wide range of applications [5]. This work harnesses the characteristics of green leaves-derived CD for three cutting-edge applications: photoelectrochemical cells, light-emitting diodes, and information encryption.

Photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting is a promising method to efficiently convert solar energy into hydrogen [6]. Among the different semiconductors used, ZnO with a suitable morphology provides potentially shorter charge carrier collection pathways, large surface area, and charge-steering capabilities [7]. Conversely, its light absorption in the UV region accounts only for 4 % of solar energy, and therefore, extending the absorption to the visible region for higher solar-to-hydrogen energy conversion has been widely studied [8]. However, ZnO suffers from fast electron-hole recombination. Hence, CD are being explored to overcome these disadvantages. CD have been combined with semiconductors to obtain enhanced light absorption and reduced electron-hole recombination. CD represent one of the best options among available materials to enhance the light absorption of broadband photoanodes in both the UV and visible regions [9].

Another application that can take advantage of CD optical characteristics is light-emitting diodes (LEDs). Because of the narrow wavelength emissions of bare LED, additional phosphor layers are utilized to obtain the desired spectrum of produced light [10]. Considerable advancements have been made in the development of LEDs utilizing CD as a phosphor material. This is attributed to the tuning of their luminescence, cost-effectiveness, cost-effectiveness, and ease of access [11]. The introduction of CD as a phosphor for white LEDs in 2011 represented a major milestone in the application of CD. [12]. Since then, CD have been used to produce LEDs of the desired emission colors like yellow, red, green, orange etc [13].

Apart from fluorescence characteristics, CD are being investigated as a potential candidate [14] for phosphorescence applications [15]. CD are well suited for preparing room-temperature phosphorescent materials [16] because of their broad and tunable emission, long lifetime and high stability [17]. Individual CD can suffer on high dispersion rate of excited surface states, which can be largely improved by locking the surface fluorophores, by embedding the CD into a rigid matrix. Moreover, fixation of the CD in a solid matrix excludes their Brownian motion, collisions and re-agglomerations too. Among the different matrixes used [18], boric acid-based matrix is a simple but very effective way of imparting long-life phosphorescence to the CD by improving the intersystem crossing and reducing non-radiative transitions [19]. Various potential applications of phosphorescent CD having boric acid matrix include information encryption, anti-counterfeiting, and fingerprint detection [20].

In this work, CD have been synthesized from a green source, namely chlorophyll, by solvothermal method. Chlorophyll was chosen because it is cheap and easily extractable raw material occurring in green plants. Naturally, chlorophyll consists of a porphyrin ring with a central magnesium (Mg) atom surrounded by four nitrogen atoms, which is mainly responsible for light harvesting and energy transfer [21]. Several studies have already demonstrated the efficiency of CD functionalized [22] or conjugated [23] with chlorophyll molecules. However, these hybrid materials still contain the original chlorophyll moiety, which is prone to degradation. Based on this knowledge, it is reasonable to expect generally that CD directly prepared from chlorophyll retain the basic porphyrin structural motifs inherited from the source molecules, while these structures compacted within the carbonized matrix may be more stable than their precursor. Therefore, for the first time, chlorophyll-derived CD have been investigated as an efficient photosensitizer in the form of ZnO/CD thin film in a PEC device with a potential of water-splitting application. Additionally, the CD were also tested for LED and information encryption applications using boric acid as a suitable matrix.

Baby spinach leaves were acquired from a supermarket in Zlin, Czech Republic. Absolute ethanol (≥99.8 %, M
w = 46.07 g mol−1) for chlorophyll extraction was obtained from Avantor (Czech Republic). Further, for preparation of ZnO nanorods and BACD nanocomposites, boric acid (BA, ≥99.5 %, M
w = 61.83 g mol−1), zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, M
w = 297.48 g mol−1), and polyethyleneimines (PEI, branched, average Mw ∼800 by LS) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and hexamethylenetetramine ((CH2)6N4, HMTA, M
w = 140.19 g mol−1) from Lachner. Zinc acetate dehydrate ((Zn(CH3CO2)2.2H2O, M
w = 219.49 g mol−1) was purchased from PENTA (Czech Republic) and diethanolamine ((CH2CH2OH)2NH, M
w = 105.14 g mol−1) from CDH Fine Chemicals (India). Sodium chloride (NaCl, M
w = 58.44 g mol−1) was obtained from Penta Chemicals Limited and sodium sulfate (Na2SO4, M
w = 142.04 g mol−1) was acquired from VWR chemicals. All the chemicals and solvents were used without any further purification. Deionized water with a conductivity of 0.09 μS/cm and 6.3 pH was used. Hydraulic Press H-62 was used to thin pellets of BACD.

The fluorine-doped tin oxide coated glass (FTO, surface resistivity ∼ 7 Ω/sq) used as a substrate for supported thin films was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. The UV LED diode of 310 nm was obtained from Roithner Lasertechnik (Austria).

The structural analysis of the CD was conducted using a transmission electron microscope JEM 2100 (JEOL, Japan) operated at 200 kV (LaB6 cathode, point resolution 2.3 Å) equipped with OLYMPUS SYS TENGRA camera (2048 × 2048 pixels). The particle size of the CD was estimated using ImageJ software. The crystalline structure of the ZnO film was attained on an X-ray powder diffractometer Miniflex 600 (Rigaku, Japan) with CoKα radiation (λ = 1.7903 Å), operated at a beam voltage of 40 kV and a beam current of 100 mA. The displayed XRD data are obtained after conversion from CoKα radiation to CuKα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å) using PowDLL Converter. The functional groups analysis was confirmed using a Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer Nicolet 6700 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) engaging the ATR method with the diamond crystal (4000–400 cm−1, resolution 2 cm−1, 64 scans). A UV–Vis spectrometer Lambda 1050 (PerkinElmer, US) was used for acquiring optical spectra. The photoluminescence (PL) emission studies and lifetime analysis were obtained using a spectrophotometer FLS920 (Edinburgh Instruments, UK) with a Xe lamp with a double monochromator used for excitation in a continuous-wave regime at room temperature. A pulsed diode (Model EPLED-330, Edinburgh Instruments, UK) operating at 332 nm (bandwidth 8.4 nm) was used as a source for fluorescence lifetime measurement. Xe light source shutter switch-off was used for kinetic measurements for phosphorescence decay of CD dispersed in water as well as for measurement of BACD samples, allowing distinguish between fluorescence and phosphorescence. AvaSpec 2048 fiber spectrometer (Avantes, The Netherlands) was used to record PL spectra while a laboratory lamp emitting at 302 nm was switched on and off to record the photographs of the emitting material. The spin coater Laurell WS-650Mz-23NPP was used to obtain the thin films of ZnO on the FTO substrate.

Chlorophyll was extracted from the baby spinach leaves using ethanol. In a beaker, 10 g of baby spinach leaves were mixed with 100 mL of ethanol. This mixture was stirred in a magnetic stirrer at 100 °C for 4 h. The resulting solution was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 10 min and vacuum filtered to remove large particles of leaves and obtain extracted chlorophyll filtrate of green color.

For the preparation of CD, 30 mL of the extracted chlorophyll was loaded into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and treated at 245 °C for 8 h in an oven [24]. The necessary time and temperature for full consumption of the source chlorophyll were verified by the UV–Vis absorption measurement within the initial heuristics (Fig. S1). The resulting dark brown solution was filtered using a 0.22 μm filter. The color change was examined by UV–Vis spectrometry as well. Further, the filtered solution was vacuum dried at 100 °C and then dispersed in water by adjusting to 1 mg/ml by proper sonication.

The synthesis of ZnO nanorods was achieved in two stages, as reported in the literature [25]. In the first stage, diethanolamine and zinc nitrate hexahydrate were dissolved at the equimolar concentration (0.8 M) in isopropanol and stirred at 50 °C for 1 h. After aging over the night at room temperature, the sol was spin-coated onto FTO glass substrates for 30 s at 3000 rpm. Next, the coated substrate was calcined at 500 °C for 1 h to obtain the ZnO seed layer.

In the second stage, zinc nitrate hexahydrate and HMTA were added at the equimolar concentration (0.025 M) to a beaker with 200 mL of distilled water and stirred for 10 min to achieve sufficient dissolution. After successful dispersion, 0.5 mL of PEI was added and again stirred for a few minutes. Then the solution was kept in an oven for 2 h at 95 °C. Next, the solution was poured into a 25 mL beaker containing the FTO substrate with the ZnO seed layer, and the beaker was covered using a foil and kept in the oven for 3 h at 95 °C. Finally, the ZnO nanorods on FTO substrates were obtained after proper rinsing of the films with water and drying.

For the preparation of ZnO/CD thin films, the obtained ZnO thin films on FTO substrates were immersed in 10 mL (1 mg/mL) of aqueous dispersion of CD for two, four, eight and 24 h. After the impregnation time, the films were dried in the oven at 80 °C for 2 h.

After suitable optimizations with regard to the amount of CD needed to obtain maximum phosphorescence, 2 mL of CD water dispersion were added to 3 g of boric acid in a 50 mL beaker. To this mixture, 38 mL of water was added, covered with a foil, and kept in an oven at 200 °C for 5 h. A glassy product was obtained as a result of the reaction. Finally, the product was crushed manually in a mortar and pestle to obtain powder. This powder was further used for characterizations and applications.

BACD in the form of free-standing thin films were used as a phosphor layer over LED. Different amounts of BACD, i.e. 80 mg, 113 mg, 130 mg, were compressed using a hydraulic press by applying a compression force of 60 kN to form free-standing thin films of thickness 0.454 mm (80 mg), 0.636 mm (113 mg) and 0.819 mm (130 mg). These films were then placed onto 310 nm LED, and their subsequent electroluminescence was measured.
The BACD security ink was obtained by dispersing 50 mg of BACD in 1 mL of water. This solution was then used to paint the correct information on the filter paper.

The PEC performance of the ZnO and ZnO/CD samples acting as photoelectrodes was tested using a PEC cell (spectro-EC, Redox.me). In a three-electrode configuration, the samples, Pt wire, and Ag/AgCl (saturated with NaCl) were used as the working, counter, and reference electrodes, respectively. The photoanodes (with a contact area of ∼1 cm2) were front side illuminated by a solar simulator (Pico™, G2V Optics) equipped with a standard AM 1.5G filter. The light intensity was 87.5 mW/cm2 0.5 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution was used as an electrolyte. The potentials were converted to a reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) using
V
R H E
=
V
A g / A g C l
+ 0.059
pH + 0.197
V
, where V RHE is the potential vs. RHE, V Ag/AgCl is the potential vs. Ag/AgCl electrode, and pH is the pH of the electrolyte solution.

The linear sweep voltammograms (LSV) were recorded at 5 mV/s over a potential range from −0.5 V to 1 V vs. Ag/AgCl. The incident photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) tests were measured at 1.23V vs. RHE using 12 monochromatic channels of the solar simulator. The IPCE was calculated using
I P C E =
J
p h
( λ )
× 1240
P
m o n o
( λ )
× λ
, where J ph is the photocurrent density, P mono is the monochromatic light intensity, and λ is the incident light wavelength. The applied bias photon-to-current efficiency (ABPE) was obtained using
A B P E =
J
L S V
×
(
1.23 − V
)
P
t o t a l
, where J LSV is the photocurrent density under applied potential of V, and P total is the total light intensity of the AM 1.5G solar spectrum.

The chlorophyll-based CD were effectively synthesized from spinach leaves using the low-cost and well-known solvothermal method. Chlorophyll extracted in ethanol was used as the sole precursor for the synthesis of the CD. The color change of the green precursor to brown product observed during 8 h of the synthesis at 245 °C is caused by complete thermal degradation of chlorophyll and its transformation in CD of a high carbonization degree [26]. The corresponding UV–Vis absorption spectra are presented in the supplement, Fig. S1. The scheme representing the extraction of chlorophyll and the synthesis of CD is illustrated in Fig. 1 .

The characteristics and applications of the synthesized CD have been studied thoroughly to assess their properties considering the natural source of chlorophyll and show their potential in three areas of application, i.e. photoelectrochemistry, LED performance, and information encryption.

The morphology of CD was analyzed with TEM. As can be observed in Fig. 2 a, the particles are found to have an almost spherical shape with slight deviations. The particle size distribution of the CD with the average particle size of ∼6 ± 0.7 nm is shown in Fig. 2b using a histogram. The size of the obtained particles is typical for CD and its distribution also agrees with the results described in the literature in similar case [27].

The functional groups present in the synthesized CD originate mostly from the chlorophyll structural template, as no other compounds were added to a reaction vessel during the CD synthesis. Therefore, the FTIR spectrum of the CD displayed in Fig. 3 a contains spectral bands of CD carbonized from chlorophyll after thermal decomposition. As can be seen, the broad peak at 3330 cm−1 indicates the presence of stretching of O–H groups. Stretching vibrations of unsaturated and saturated Car-/C–H groups can be observed at 3015–2850 cm−1 [28]. The group of spectral bands at around 1740–1594 cm−1 shows the presence of CO/CC/Car-Car bonds [24], the band at 1472 cm−1 characterizes C–H bending, and the sharp spectral band at 1365 cm−1 represents O–H bending. The occurrence of a spectral band at 1041 cm−1 might be related to the presence of C–O [22]. This confirms the presence of OH, CH, and CO functional groups on the surface of CD.

The optical properties of CD were investigated using the UV absorption and the PL emission spectroscopy. As can be seen in Fig. 3b, the shoulder peak observed at 275 nm in the absorption spectrum corresponds to π-π* of the CC bonds. The peak spotted at 400 nm is probably due to the n-π* transition of the CO bonds [28]. The emission characteristics of the CD were studied using the room temperature PL spectra, which were obtained with an excitation wavelength of 380 nm and with a concentration of 0.02 μg/mL of CD in water. As can be seen in Fig. 3b, when excited at 380 nm, the chlorophyll precursor has emission maxima centered mainly at two regions, at 679 nm and at 727 nm, which are typical for chlorophyll structure [29]. However, the CD emission peak is observed at 468 nm, which indicates that the original chlorophyll was successfully converted to the carbogenic matrix of the CD [30]. Moreover, the shape of the emission band and its broadening to longer wavelengths hints at the convolution of peaks arising from different emission centers [31], also related to the functional groups created on the CD surface [32]. The quantum yield of the CD dispersed in water was found to be ∼1 %. The emission map of CD (see Fig. S2) shows the typical redshift dependence of emission maximum wavelength with a longer excitation wavelength. This phenomenon is associated with (i) particle size distribution, (ii) emission centers distribution, especially distribution of surface states, and their (iii) various relaxation pathways, or even (iv) enabled by the engagement of discrete multiple electronic states [33] emerging from different types of CD aggregates in the solution [34].

Fig. 4 comprises an analysis of the emission maximum shift in dependence on the used excitation wavelength. The dependence is linear with a slope 0.668 ± 0.025 and intersect at 0.53 ± 0.09. The difference between λ
exc and λ
em diminishes from 0,78 to 0,49 eV. The extrapolation of the data to the zero difference between excitation and emission maximum energies yields a value of (1.6 ± 0.2) eV which corresponds to 775 nm. This value indicates the lower boundary of the emission state distribution and the minimum width of the forbidden gap. This estimate corresponds well to the longest observable room temperature PL emission wavelengths of the parent chlorophyll molecule, confirming thus the inheritance of the source structural motifs to the CD structure. The average lifetime of CD was calculated using a double exponential decay. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the average fluorescence lifetime of the CD observed at 410 nm was found to be 4.1 ns when excited by 332 nm excitation pulsed diode. Moreover, the CD also demonstrated a phosphorescence in their dispersed state at the emission wavelength of 450 nm when excited at 300 nm. The phosphorescence decay of the CD in water was found to be monoexponential, and its lifetime, as depicted in Fig. 5b, was found to be about 100 ms. This might be due to intermolecular interactions of surface states and water bound on the CD [35].

Further, the CD were embedded in the boric acid-based matrix to improve phosphorescence lifetime. A boric acid-based matrix is one of the most suitable matrices for obtaining good phosphorescence characteristics, as it does not manifest any inherent absorption or emission and the process of its preparation and particle embedding has already been investigated and well established [36]. The UV spectra of the BACD and blank sample in Fig. 6 a depict no absorption of the heated boric acid in the blank. Therefore, only the UV spectra of the CD in BACD are observed (similar to Fig. 3b). The absorption peak at 400 nm is significantly pronounced, possibly due to the interaction of the chromophores with the solid matrix. Fig. 6b shows the PL spectra of the BACD at 302 nm, where one main peak and a massive shoulder peak are observed at 478 nm and 587 nm, respectively. The emission is widely extended towards the red end of the visible spectra, covering thus its full range, resulting in the white emission. The PL quantum yield of BACD was found to be around 4 %. Compared to free CD in the liquid environment, the increase in the quantum yield testifies that the opening of the phosphorescence emission channel (in the range 525–625 nm) was not at the expense of fluorescence intensity but was caused by reducing non-radiative relaxation processes. Generally, the stabilization of CD in the solid matrix promotes singlet–triplet inter-system crossing and inhibits the non-radiative transitions, thus leading to effective phosphorescence [19]. As displayed in Fig. 6c, when excited at 302 nm, the BACD exhibited naked eye phosphorescence of 10 s. The phosphorescence decay observed in Fig. 6d was found to be monoexponential with a lifetime of the BACD estimated to be 1.5 s, which is a substantial improvement over individual CD in the water dispersion. The longer phosphorescence lifetime in BACD is mainly because of the formation of B2O3 and other phases obtained by the dehydration of boric acid. When CD are added to boric acid and thermally treated, the CD are embedded in a solid matrix. The rigid environment formed around CD plays a crucial role. It restricts the vibrational and rotational non-radiative relaxation of the functional groups, which leads to successful intersystem crossing of electrons from singlet to triplet states, thereby enhancing the phosphorescence emission due to increased radiative transition of triplet excitons. The matrix also prevents aggregation-induced quenching of the CD in the solid state, enhancing phosphorescence as well [36]. The FTIR spectrum in Fig. S3 provided no additional information about the CD structural changes in BACD due to the low loading of CD.

Thin films of zinc oxide and CD were deposited on the FTO substrate using a multistep procedure described in the experimental section to study the effect of CD as a photosensitizer on ZnO. To gain an understanding of the morphology, TEM images of ZnO nanorods in the presence and absence of CD were taken, and they are depicted in Fig. 7 . As shown in Fig. 7a, the rods in the absence of CD appear smooth with clear edges without any signs of particles and have a diameter of around 273 nm. However, as can be observed in Fig. 7b, the ZnO films impregnated with CD for 2h bear the spherical-shaped particles of CD on the surface of ZnO nanorods, which indicates an efficient impregnation procedure and creation of heterostructured ZnO/CD. Increased impregnation time causes CD agglomeration, preventing CD from individually, and thus effectively, binding onto the ZnO surface. This causes the CD to be seen as separate from the ZnO nanorods (Fig. S4). The XRD diffractogram of ZnO is given in Fig. S5, and the sharp peak (002) at 35° confirms the perpendicular growth of the ZnO nanorods on the FTO substrate [25].

The optical characteristics of the ZnO/CD thin films were further studied using UV–Vis and PL spectroscopy because light absorption capacity is one of the key elements influencing the PEC performance of electrode materials. As can be seen in Fig. 8 a, the typical absorption edge of the ZnO nanorod film was found to be ∼360 nm, corresponding to a bandgap of 3.44 eV [37]. While ZnO/CD film evinces an increase in absorption intensity with the same absorption edge around 360 nm. Additionally, an increase in absorption intensity is observed at 400 nm extended up to 500 nm due to n-π* transitions of the CO bonds in CD, corroborating well with the data observed in Fig. 3b. Hence, CD play a critical role in increasing both the UV and the visible light absorption of the ZnO/CD thin films.

As illustrated in Fig. 8b, when excited at 350 nm, the PL emission spectra of the ZnO nanorods show peaks in both the UV and the visible region. The peak observed at ∼380 nm is ascribed to the near band-edge emission, and the broad peak observed at 620 nm is due to the existence of crystal defects such as oxygen vacancies and Zn interstitials, mostly surface defects [38]. When impregnated with CD and excited at 350 nm, the emission wavelength and intensity at 380 nm remain the same, however, there is increased emission intensity at 620 nm. This increase indicates interaction between the surface defect states and associated CD, which can be suitable for the transport of the holes to ZnO–electrolyte interface for the water oxidation activity, thereby increasing the photocurrent density [39].

PEC measurements were performed to investigate the photoinduced charge transfer between CD and the ZnO thin films. Fig. 9 a displays linear sweep voltammetry of ZnO/CD and pristine ZnO under light on-off cycles. The photocurrent response of the two electrodes increases with increasing applied bias and no current saturation was observed. In the light-off cycles, ZnO/CD produced a very negligible current of ∼0.01 mA/cm2 due to the non-faradaic reaction, indicating that the observed current is merely generated by the photoexcited charge carriers. At 1.23 V vs RHE, ZnO/CD exhibited a photocurrent of 0.225 mA/cm2, which is almost 3 times higher than that of pristine ZnO (0.09 mA/cm2). The higher photoactivity of ZnO/CD heterostructure implies that molecular interaction between CD and ZnO plays a critical role. The enhanced PEC performance can be attributed to the two major advancements. First, the incorporation of CD on the surface of ZnO significantly extended the light-harvesting capacity towards the visible range. Second, the heterostructure between the CD and ZnO provides a viable transfer of the photogenerated electrons from CD to ZnO [40]. In line with our results, Xu et al. also reported that ZnO nanorods photoanodes functionalized with CD demonstrated a better solar energy harvesting ability, boosting the overall PEC water-splitting performance [41]. Fig. 9b shows the LSV curves of ZnO/CD at different impregnation times (2, 4, 8, and 24 h). The observed photocurrent tends to decrease with the duration of immersion of the ZnO films in the CD solution. Among all, ZnO/CD with an impregnation time of 2 h produced the highest and the optimized photocurrent of 0.225 mA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE. Since many photodevices are designed for illumination through the overlayer, there is a trade-off between parasitic light absorption, the surface sensitizer, and the PEC activity. It should be emphasized that the light absorption range of CD (around 400 nm) is highly likely to overlap with that of ZnO. Thus, it is anticipated that the excess of CD on the surface of ZnO could decrease the number of photons reaching ZnO, and in return, the PEC response is adversely affected. Moreover, agglomeration of CD for longer impregnation periods was observed, which has an adverse effect on the device function. Fig. 9c indicates the photostability of the pristine and ZnO/CD thin films over a period of 30 min. After 30 min, the photocurrent of the ZnO/CD decreased by 32 % at 0.6 V. However, it still exhibited high photocurrent compared to the pristine ZnO films. This indicates a good photostability of the ZnO/CD. A sudden increase, followed by an immediate decrease, and then an almost stable photocurrent is observed in ZnO/CD. We believe that interactions between the water and organic residues on the thin film might lead to an unusual spike. With time the spike disappears, and a steady-state photocurrent is achieved from the ZnO/CD film.

The charge transfer kinetics of the developed electrodes were studied using EIS at 1.23 V under AM 1.5 G irradiation. The EIS results in the form of a Nyquist plot are depicted in Fig. 10 a. The dots in the graph indicate the experimental results, and the solid lines show the output of fitting the experimental results with the equivalent circuit model (simplified Randle's cell) shown in the inset of the EIS plot. In the circuit model, Rs represents the resistance of the electrolyte solution, Rct represents the charge transfer resistance, and CPE represents the Constant phase element. The diameter of the semicircle in the Nyquist plot is associated with the interfacial charge carrier resistance, governing the charge transfer kinetics at the electrode/electrolyte interface. The larger Rct means poorer reaction kinetics. The Rct value of ZnO/CD thin films (6537 Ω) is substantially lower compared to that of pristine ZnO thin films (43393 Ω), indicating a faster charge transfer. Carbon quantum dots were proposed as promising hole transport layer over [42] wide band gap base materials [43]. On this basis, it is discernible that the incorporation of CD expedites the hole extraction from ZnO as a main absorbing layer, avoiding the surface recombination of the photogenerated carriers and promoting the water oxidation kinetics at the interface between the photoelectrode and electrolyte. Another reason for the lower Rct could be the broader light absorption range after CD functionalization, leading to the formation of more electron-hole pairs.

The spectral light response that impacts the total PEC activity of the constructed photoanodes was identified using the IPCE efficiency test. Fig. 10b shows the IPCE spectra of ZnO/CD and pristine ZnO thin films. Both samples exhibited a drastically decreasing IPCE trend around 400 nm, as expected from ZnO-based photoanodes. The IPCE response from CD/ZnO red shifts towards the visible region of the spectrum compared to the pristine ZnO, which is in good agreement with their absorption spectra (Fig. 8a). This result signifies that there is a feasible transfer of photoinduced electrons from CD to ZnO, contributing to the overall PEC response. A similar IPCE enhancement for ZnO [44], as well as other wide band gap metal oxide functionalized with the carbon quantum dots photosensitizer, was also obtained in the other previous studies [45].

The ABPE spectra of the ZnO/CD thin films and pristine ZnO is illustrated in Fig. 10c. The CD sensitization on the ZnO resulted in a 1.5-fold enhancement in the ABPE value (0.047 %) in comparison to that of the pristine ZnO (0.0317 %). Besides, the ABPE curve suggested that the onset potential of the CD/ZnO appeared to be slightly lower than ZnO. It should be noted that the overpotential losses arising from the photoanode are involved in the ABPE values, whereas the losses associated with the ionic resistance are not considered. The IPCE, ABPE and EIS results are in good harmony with the observed PEC water-splitting performance ranking of the fabricated photoelectrodes.

The PEC water splitting mechanism within ZnO/CD photoelectrode is proposed and illustrated in Fig. 11 . The PEC process begins with the excitation of the electron-holes pairs when the light is available on the photoanode surface. The photoexcited electrons in the conduction band (CB) of ZnO are driven towards the Pt electrode through the FTO conductive substrate to run the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) while the photogenerated holes in the valence band (VB) of ZnO are injected to run oxygen evolution reaction (OER). In the meanwhile, the photoexcited electrons in the Fermi level of CD can travel to the CB of ZnO [46] to increase the charge carrier density of the heterostructure [47]. The presence of the CD extends the light absorption towards the visible region and enhances the overall PEC water-splitting performance by injecting photogenerated electrons into the conduction band of ZnO. However, the CD can also serve as an electron reservoir to trap photoexcited electrons from ZnO, enhancing thus the charge separation [48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: BACD films as phosphor in LEDs
ce:section.ce:para: BACD were found to have successful application as a phosphor material in LED applications. Solid-state LED was fabricated by producing free-standing thin films of BACD. Different weights of BACD were taken and pressed in a mold with a nominal force of 60 kN. The obtained films had a thickness of 0.454 mm (A), 0.636 mm (B) and 0.819 mm (C). The films were then placed on top of a 310 nm LED, and their subsequent electroluminescence characteristics were recorded. The voltage used for conducting the experiment was 5 V, which is suitable for indoor lighting. The attained luminescence using the BACD films can be seen clearly in Fig. 12 a. The free-standing BACD films are able to absorb the UV light from the LED and convert them to visible light. As can be observed, with the increased thickness of the films, a reduction in the luminous intensity is attained. However, it is also noteworthy that increased thickness leads to a possible shift in the emission wavelength towards a visible region. The color of the light-emitting diodes after using phosphor material is illustrated using a chromaticity diagram in Fig. 12b. The attained CIE 1931 x, y coordinates of the phosphor films are A(0.3,0.37), B(0.31,0.37) C(0.33,0.38), which corresponds to the color temperatures of 6820 K, 6389 K, and 5588 K, respectively. The color rendering index (CRI) value, calculated from the spectrum was 70. This indicates that BACD can be used as a promising white-light-emitting phosphor material for UV LEDs.

The phosphorescence potential of BACD was also utilized for promising information encryption. The BACD were converted into security ink, and the application is demonstrated in Fig. 13 . A microarray data storage pattern according to the binary codes of the standard 8-bit ASCII characters is also shown in Fig. 13. The information was prepared using filter paper. The filter paper contains information about two binary codes, “1 and 0”. The information “1” on the filter paper is obtained by simply painting the filter paper with the BACD ink, and the uncoated filter paper contains the information “0”.

The arrangement of the information is represented as a Goose code. When the UV light 302 nm is on, the Goose data is obtained, where there is no distinction in the information, and all appear the same as “1”. However, when the light is switched off, the correct information “1” is observed in the form of luminescent filter paper due to the phosphorescence characteristics of BACD ink. The filter papers that are not luminescent after switching off the UV light contain the information ”0”. This correct information after the switching off of the light is denoted as “Real Data”. The real data is read and decoded into “01010100, 01000010, 01010101” and eventually decoded to their ASCII binary characters as “T B U” representing the acronym of Tomas Bata University in Zlín.

The present study reports on the bottom-up synthesis of CD from chlorophyll, which has a rich porphyrin structure with highly efficient light harvesting characteristics. Baby spinach leaves were used as a chlorophyll source, which was then transformed into CD using a cost-effective solvothermal method while keeping residual structural motifs embedded in the carbonized matrix as confirmed by the room temperature PL studies. The particle size distribution is relatively narrow, centered about 6 nm in diameter. Prepared CD exhibit typical excitation wavelength dependence of the emission light intensity maximum, possibly associated with surface state distribution and relaxation mechanism, as evident from their significant long-lived phosphorescence after being locked to a rigid matrix. The CD were proven as an efficient photosensitizer material with ZnO when coated onto ZnO using the impregnation method at different time intervals. A high current density of 0.225 mA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE was obtained with a 2-h impregnation time, thus enhancing the current density of the unmodified ZnO photoanode three times. The ZnO/CD was able to provide a significantly higher current efficiency due to enhanced light absorption extended above 400 nm and improved interfacial charge transfer kinetics. Furthermore, the PL properties of the chlorophyll-derived CD were further leveraged to obtain phosphor material, i.e., BACD nanocomposite, by incorporation of the CD into a boric acid-derived matrix. Prepared BACD exhibited excellent naked eye room temperature phosphorescence of 10 s and average room temperature phosphorescence lifetime of 1.5 s. The free-standing BACD film was mounted on a UV (310 nm) LED excitation source, enabling visible intense white light emission of color temperatures controlled with the thickness of the film. Moreover, the BACD composite was successfully applied as a phosphorescent ink suitable for information encryption controlled by a UV light on-off switch. Hence, the chlorophyll-based CD originating from a green and renewable plant source successfully manifested their potential to be utilized for multiple advanced energy and photo/electronic applications.

R. Blessy Pricilla: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Ali Can Guler: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Pavel Urbanek: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Data curation. Michal Urbanek: Data curation. Raz Jelinek: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Barbora Hanulikova: Writing – review & editing. Ivo Kuritka: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
The datasets generated and analyzed during the current study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are happy to acknowledge the support given by the Ministry of Education, Youth and Sports of the Czech Republic DKRVO (RP/CPS/2024-28/007). Ali Can Güler also expresses his gratitude for support within the “Creativity, Intelligence & Talent for the Zlín Region” (CIT - ZK) program.
